cards.title: Grammar school pressure: Do kids really need tutors?
cards.body: A parent worries their daughter can't get into grammar school without expensive tutoring. An advice columnist suggests the child may have different needs and that parental support matters more than test prep.

cards.title: Only children aren't weird—and parents aren't selfish
cards.body: Stereotypes about only children being lonely and maladjusted persist, but research shows these prejudices are unfounded. Parents choosing to have one child are increasingly common and doing just fine.

cards.title: Does your baby need a pension? The new parenting guilt
cards.body: Parents are being encouraged to open self-invested personal pensions for children. But with 15 million adults struggling to save for their own retirement, is this realistic parenting advice?

cards.title: Not all screen time is created equal
cards.body: Interactive screens that respond to children can teach critical thinking, while passive screens may do the opposite. Understanding screen quality matters more than total screen time.

cards.title: Intergenerational trauma: Understanding and healing family patterns
cards.body: Experiences like incarcerated Japanese Americans during WWII show how trauma passes through generations. Understanding these patterns helps parents break cycles and heal their families.

cards.title: Your discipline struggles may trace back to childhood
cards.body: Struggling with self-discipline isn't a character flaw—it often reflects what you didn't receive growing up. Parents can identify and build these missing foundations for themselves and their children.

cards.title: Music and movement help children find their rhythm
cards.body: A daughter reflects on how her father used music and steady presence to help his three children develop their own rhythms. This creates a shared family beat while respecting individuality.

cards.title: Routine mental health screenings could prevent school crises
cards.body: Schools often search for missed warning signs after tragedies, but proactive mental health screening could identify struggling students earlier. Prevention is more effective than crisis response.
